column1: What is an Inverted Index?;"Maps terms → document IDs that contain them. Instead of scanning every document for a query term
column2: you look it up and get the matching docs instantly.

column1: Forward index answers: ""What words are in doc X?""
Inverted index answers: ""What docs contain word X?""
Search engines use inverted indexes because queries ask for documents by term
What is a Posting List?;"The list of document IDs stored for a term in the inverted index. When you look up a term
Posting lists are kept sorted by doc ID — this enables efficient intersection (AND queries) using a linear merge instead of nested loops."

What is Tokenization in a search engine?;"Splitting raw text into individual units (tokens) for indexing and querying. Both documents and queries go through the same tokenization so they use the same vocabulary.
tokens = text.split()  # splits on any whitespace
What are Stopwords and why remove them?;"Stopwords are common words with little semantic meaning: ""the""
- They appear in almost every document → low discriminative power

column2: check membership before indexing each token."

column1: What is Stemming and why use it in search?;"Stemming reduces words to their root form so that different grammatical forms of the same word match each other.
A query for ""running"" should match documents about ""run"" or ""runs"".
Stemming improves recall (finding more relevant results).
Trade-off: loses tense and plurality nuance (""ran"" ≠ ""run"" after stemming in some cases)."
What is Term Frequency (TF)?;"TF = how many times a term appears in a specific document. A higher TF means the term is more central to that document's topic.

column2: count each token using a Counter.

column1: term_frequencies[doc_id].update(tokens)  # counts all tokens at once
Used in: TF-IDF and BM25 scoring."
What is Document Frequency (DF)?;"DF = the number of documents in the corpus that contain a given term.
DF(""the"")   ≈ N  (appears in almost all docs → low signal)
DF(""heist"") = 3  (appears in few docs → high signal)
Used in: IDF and BM25 IDF calculations.
df = len(index.get_documents(term))  # length of the posting list"
What is IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) and what formula do we use?;"IDF measures how rare a term is across all documents. Rare terms are more informative for search.
df = number of documents containing the term
+1 = smoothing to avoid division by zero
Common term (""the""):  df ≈ N → IDF ≈ 0   (not useful)
Rare term (""heist""):  df = 3 → IDF is high (strong signal)
What is IDF smoothing and why do we add +1?;"Adding a small constant to numerator and denominator before computing IDF.
1. df = 0 (term not in any doc) → division by zero without smoothing
2. df = N (term in every doc)   → log(1) = 0
3. Reduces extreme values at the edges"
What is TF-IDF and when is the score high?;"TF-IDF scores how important a term is to a specific document relative to the whole corpus.
- Term appears often in this document (high TF)
- Term is rare across all documents (high IDF)
→ The term is uniquely characteristic of this document

column2: e.g. ""the"") — even if TF is high

column1: - Term barely appears in the document (low TF)
What is BM25?;"BM25 (Best Match 25) is a modern relevance ranking function used in search engines (e.g. Elasticsearch
It improves over TF-IDF in two ways:
1. Saturating TF — more occurrences give diminishing returns (not linear)
2. Better IDF — stays non-negative even for very common terms
BM25 score for a document given a query:

column2: doc) × BM25_IDF(term)   for each query term

column1: The ""25"" refers to the 25th iteration in a series of retrieval experiments (Okapi BM25)."
What formula does BM25 TF use and what does k1 control?;"Formula:
k1 controls TF saturation — how quickly extra occurrences stop adding value.
Compare to raw TF: grows to infinity linearly with no cap.
Why does BM25 TF saturate and why is that useful?;"In raw TF
column2: going from 1→2 occurrences has the same impact as 100→101. This is unrealistic — the first few occurrences carry most of the signal.

column1: BM25 TF applies diminishing returns: each extra occurrence adds less weight than the previous
- Prevents very long documents from dominating just because a term appears many times
- More accurately reflects human intuition: ""appears often"" ≠ ""appears infinitely more relevant"""
What formula does BM25 IDF use and how does it differ from standard IDF?;"Formula:
Key difference: BM25 IDF is always ≥ 0.
Standard IDF goes negative when df > N/2 (term in more than half the docs).
BM25 IDF stays non-negative because of the + 1 outside the log — safe for ranking.
What is the difference between AND and OR query semantics in search?;"AND (intersection): document must contain ALL query terms.
OR (union): document needs ANY query term.

column3: can return zero if one rare term is missing

column1: Production engines typically use OR for retrieval (find candidates)
column2: then rank by how many terms matched and scoring (BM25)."

What is index persistence in a search engine and why split data into multiple files?;"Index persistence = saving the built index to disk so it doesn't need to be rebuilt on every run.
index.pkl            → term → set of doc IDs
docmap.pkl           → doc ID → full document data
term_frequencies.pkl → doc ID → term counts (needed for TF/BM25)
- Load only what's needed for a given operation
- Rebuild parts independently (e.g. reindex without reloading raw data)
What is the purpose of a "seen" set during search result collection?;"When multiple query tokens match the same document
column2: that document could appear multiple times in results. A seen set prevents duplicates.

column1: Without seen: a doc matching both ""police"" and ""crime"" appears twice.
With seen: it appears exactly once.
Note: this OR approach doesn't rank docs matching more terms higher. A scoring function (BM25) would fix that."
